query: effectiveness of aptiganel in stroke treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cerebral Artery Stroke -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Beyond the acute management of stroke, the use of antihypertensives, dual antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulants, and carotid endarterectomy should be used to prevent recurrent events. Antiplatelet therapy or anticoagulants are not recommended within 24h after alteplase administration. Aspirin is not a recommendation as a substitute for other interventions for acute stroke. Administration of a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor inhibitor is not recommended, and a recent Cochrane review showed that these agents correlated with a high risk of intracranial hemorrhage. Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel) is recommended to start within 24 hours for 21 days in patients with minor stroke for early secondary stroke prevention. The CHANCE trial showed that the primary outcome of a recurrent stroke at 90 days favored dual antiplatelet therapy over aspirin alone (HR 0.68; 95% CI, 0.57 to 0.81, p<0.0001). Ticagrelor over aspirin in acute stroke treatment is not recommended. According to SOCRATES trial with the primary outcome of time to the composite endpoint of stroke, myocardial infarction (MI) or death up to 90 days, ticagrelor was not found to be superior to aspirin (hazard ratio [HR] 0.89, 95% CI, 0.78-1.01; p=0.07). However, ticagrelor is a reasonable alternative in patients with contraindication to aspirin. The efficacy of tirofiban and eptifibatide is currently unknown. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Anticoagulation(Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The guidelines also recommend further clinical trials and suggest a lack of well-established data for the use of direct thrombin inhibitors and factor Xa inhibitors, including argatroban, dabigatran, etc., as monotherapy or adjunctive therapy to alteplase in AIS. [11] The studies demonstrating the safety and efficacy of using thrombin inhibitors or factor Xa inhibitors have small populations, limiting the applicability of the results. The safety and feasibility of dabigatran within 24 hours of minor strokes or transient ischemic attacks (TIA), defined as NIHSS score less than or equal to 3, was studied in N=53 patients. The study found that initiating dabigatran was feasible as no patients experienced a symptomatic hemorrhagic stroke; however, a larger randomized controlled trial is needed to confirm safety. [22] Another study looked at the safety of direct thrombin inhibitor, argatroban, in conjunction with tPA. The open-label, pilot study, known as ARTSS-2 (Argatroban with Recombinant Tissue Plasminogen Activator for Acute Stroke), included N=90 patients and found that rates of intracerebral hemorrhage were comparable among control and study arm groups; however, a larger trial is needed to determine efficacy. [23] Additionally, a post hoc analysis from a multicenter prospective study, CROMIS-2 (Clinical Relevance of Microbleeds in Stroke-2), reviewed initiation of anticoagulation timing post-ischemic strokes and looked at 90-day clinical outcomes. The results included N=1355 patients and found that initiation of oral anticoagulation (OAC) early-on (0 to 4 days) vs. later (greater than or equal to 5 days or never started) post-stroke associated with atrial fibrillation had similar odds for the composite endpoint of ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, and death at 90 days. [23] However, the data is still insufficient to change practice as adequately powered controlled randomized trials are still needed. [11] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Indications -- Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: A 2009 study published in the New England Journal of Medicine compared the combination of ASA-ERDP with clopidogrel for preventing the risk of recurrent stroke. [9] This double-blinded study evaluated patients receiving aspirin 25 mg with extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg twice daily versus clopidogrel 75 mg once daily. The study demonstrated comparable outcomes in the primary endpoint of stroke recurrence, with similar rates between groups: 8.8% in the clopidogrel group and 9.0% in the ASA-ERDP group. The results indicated that neither treatment was superior in preventing stroke recurrence.

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: Several randomized controlled trials for acute ischemic stroke have compared tenecteplase and alteplase. The EXTEND-1A TNK trial showed better reperfusion versus alteplase in lesions with low clot burden. [13] The NOR-TEST2 trial with 0.4 mg/kg of tenecteplase failed to show non-inferiority to a standard dose of IV alteplase. [14] The ATTEST trial compared 0.25 mg/kg of tenecteplase and the standard dose of IV alteplase and found no differences in the outcomes. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: From all the available data about tenecteplase in acute ischemic stroke, one can conclude that no clear positive phase 3 trial shows its superiority over alteplase. 0.4 mg/kg of tenecteplase showed no advantage over the 0.25 mg/kg dose. Given its longer half-life so that it can be given as a single bolus dose and its lower cost, tenecteplase is becoming more accepted in most stroke centers in the USA over alteplase.

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue Plasminogen Activator Therapy -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: P2Y12 inhibitors: P2Y12 inhibitors (such as clopidogrel, prasugrel, and ticagrelor) may be initiated after alteplase administration for myocardial infarction or as part of secondary stroke prevention following thrombolysis. However, concurrent use during or immediately after alteplase infusion is generally avoided due to the increased risk of bleeding. In stroke patients, delayed initiation—typically 24 hours or more after alteplase administration—may be considered once follow-up neuroimaging confirms the absence of intracranial hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue Plasminogen Activator Therapy -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: In clinical studies involving adult patients with acute ischemic stroke ( n =624), the incidence of intracranial bleeding—particularly symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage—was significantly higher in patients treated with alteplase compared to those receiving placebo (total intracranial bleeding: 15.4% versus 6.4%, P <.01; symptomatic intracranial bleeding: 8% versus 1.3%, P <.01). Despite this increased risk, there was no corresponding rise in 90-day mortality or severe disability among patients treated with alteplase. Studies indicate that the incidence of intracranial bleeding is dose-dependent, with the highest incidence observed at a dose of 150 mg (1.3%) compared to 100 mg (0.4%). Overall, the rate of adverse events, including bleeding, appears to correlate with the total cumulative exposure to tPA. Cardiac dysrhythmias may occur during tPA administration for non-STEMI and are typically related to the restoration of tissue perfusion rather than direct drug effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue Plasminogen Activator Therapy -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Other fibrinolytics: Sequential or combined use of alteplase with other fibrinolytic agents (such as streptokinase, tenecteplase, reteplase, and urokinase) is contraindicated due to additive fibrinolytic effects and an increased risk of severe bleeding and hemorrhagic stroke. Alteplase should not be administered within 24 hours of treatment with another fibrinolytic.

retrieved_docs.title: Alteplase -- Administration -- Clinical Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: The NINDS trial was a randomized trial of IV alteplase versus placebo in patients with acute ischemic stroke treated within 3 hours of symptom onset. Alteplase improved functional outcomes at three months in comparison to placebo. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Indications -- Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: Further research has shown that, similar to aspirin and clopidogrel, ASA-ERDP is recommended for the secondary, long-term prevention of non-cardioembolic strokes. [10] This combination was also found to be safer and more efficient than aspirin alone in small vessel disease. [11] As per the 2021 guidelines from the American Heart Association and the American Stroke Association, antiplatelet therapy is recommended over oral anticoagulation for patients with non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke or TIA. This approach aims to lower the risk of recurrent ischemic stroke and other cardiovascular issues while also reducing bleeding risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tenecteplase has emerged as the preferred thrombolytic agent for the management of acute myocardial infarction due to its efficacy, convenience, and cost-effectiveness compared to the FDA-approved alteplase. While tenecteplase is not FDA-approved for acute ischemic strokes, its off-label use is increasing due to its favorable attributes such as single-dose intravenous administration and lower cost when compared to alteplase.Tenecteplase, administered at the stroke dosage of 0.25 mg/kg up to 25 mg, is a more cost-effective option compared to alteplase for stroke treatment in the US market, with a cost difference of approximately $3000. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Alteplase -- Administration -- Clinical Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: In the ECASS trial, alteplase administered between 3 and 4.5 hours after onset of acute ischemic stroke symptoms resulted in a lower incidence of the primary end-point, disability at three months, compared to placebo. However, alteplase was more frequently associated with symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage than placebo.

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Indications -- Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: For secondary prevention of ischemic stroke, recommended options include aspirin (50-325 mg) daily, clopidogrel (75 mg) daily, or the combination of aspirin (25 mg) and extended-release dipyridamole (200 mg) twice daily. [12] [13] In patients with small subcortical infarction, a network meta-analysis of 19 randomized controlled trials demonstrated that cilostazol significantly reduced the incidence of major adverse cardiovascular events compared with other antiplatelet therapies. [14] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: The most recently published AcT trial in Canada is a large randomized open-label trial comparing 0.25 mg/kg of tenecteplase and alteplase. This trial demonstrated the non-inferiority of tenecteplase versus alteplase. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Indications -- Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: Stroke prevention: The indication for stroke prevention is primarily based on data obtained from the European Stroke Prevention Study 2 Trial (ESPS-2). [7] This randomized controlled trial evaluated stroke risk reduction in patients with recent transient ischemic attacks (TIA) or ischemic stroke. The patients meeting the inclusion criteria were categorized into 4 distinct treatment groups: placebo treatment, aspirin 25 mg twice daily, extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg twice daily, or a combination of aspirin 25 mg and extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg twice daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Lacunar Stroke -- Treatment / Management -- Lacunar Stroke Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of a patient who presents with acute lacunar infarct out of the tPA window consistent with a noncardioembolic stroke includes dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel within 24 hours of symptom onset and continued for 21 days. Dual antiplatelet therapy in the acute phase effectively lowers recurrent ischemic stroke for 90 days from symptom onset. This is supported by the Clopidogrel in High-Risk Patients with Acute Nondisabling Cerebrovascular Events (CHANCE) and the Platelet-Oriented Inhibition in New TIA and Minor Ischemic Stroke (POINT) trials. These studies were performed on patients with all types of ischemic stroke but supported for use in lacunar strokes in subgroup analysis. Antiplatelet therapy should be delayed for 24 hours in patients who received tPA. [34] Patients with stuttering lacunar infarcts may also benefit from intravenous tPA.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 668
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excluding heparin constitute a therapeutic class that includes beraprost, dipyridamole, indobufen, clopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. All of these agents share the primary function of inhibiting platelet aggregation through distinct pharmacologic mechanisms, making them useful alternatives to heparin for preventing thrombus formation in various cardiovascular and cerebrovascular conditions. By targeting platelet activation pathways, these drugs help reduce the risk of arterial occlusion, myocardial infarction, stroke, and other thromboembolic events, and they are selected based on specific patient factors, drug‑specific actions, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class that includes heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin, as well as antithrombin III. All of these agents share the common mechanism of potentiating antithrombin activity to inhibit clot formation and are used to prevent and treat thromboembolic disorders. In clinical practice, heparin may be employed in the management of cerebral infarction (احتشاء دماغي), underscoring the therapeutic relevance of this drug class for acute stroke and other conditions where rapid anticoagulation is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2251
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excluding heparin represent a pharmacologic class that encompasses a variety of agents used to prevent platelet clumping in cardiovascular and thrombotic disorders. Members of this class include beraprost, dipyridamol, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. Each of these drugs exerts antiplatelet effects through distinct mechanisms, yet they share the common therapeutic goal of reducing platelet aggregation without the anticoagulant properties of heparin. Understanding that beraprost, dipyridamol, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil are all categorized as platelet aggregation inhibitors excl. heparin helps clinicians select appropriate antiplatelet therapy tailored to individual patient needs while avoiding the use of heparin‑based agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1573
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excl. heparin is a pharmacologic class that encompasses a variety of agents used to prevent platelet clumping and thrombus formation without involving heparin. Members of this class include beraprost, dipyridamol, epoprostenol, iloprost, klopidogrel (likely referring to clopidogrel), selexipag, and ticagrelor. Each of these drugs exerts antiplatelet effects through distinct mechanisms—such as prostacyclin analogues (beraprost, epoprostenol, iloprost), phosphodiesterase inhibition (dipyridamol), P2Y12 receptor antagonism (klopidogrel, ticagrelor), and selective prostacyclin receptor agonism (selexipag). By inhibiting platelet aggregation, they are employed in the management and prevention of cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events, offering therapeutic options that differ from heparin’s anticoagulant action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excl. heparin is a therapeutic class that includes beraprost, dipyridamol, epoprostenol, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. All of these agents are members of this non‑heparin platelet aggregation inhibitor group and share the common purpose of reducing platelet aggregation to prevent thrombotic events. By acting through mechanisms distinct from heparin, these drugs provide alternative options for antiplatelet therapy in patients who require platelet inhibition but may not be suitable for heparin use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 393
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excluding heparin constitute a pharmacologic class that includes beraprost, dipyridamol, eptifibatid, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. Each of these agents is recognized as a member of this class and functions to inhibit platelet aggregation through distinct mechanisms, thereby helping to prevent thrombotic events. By grouping these drugs together, clinicians can differentiate them from heparin‑based anticoagulants and consider them collectively when selecting antiplatelet therapy for patients at risk of clot formation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin, an anticoagulant medication, may be employed in the management of احتشاء دماغي (cerebral infarction or stroke). By inhibiting clot formation, heparin can help prevent the propagation of thrombi in patients experiencing this type of brain injury, underscoring its therapeutic role in acute stroke care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Argatroban belongs to the class of direct thrombin inhibitors, a group of anticoagulant agents that exert their therapeutic effect by directly inhibiting the enzyme thrombin, thereby preventing the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin and reducing clot formation. This classification underscores argatroban’s mechanism of action and its role in managing conditions that require precise anticoagulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2539
retrieved_docs.content: Argatroban and dabigatran etexilát are both members of the pharmacologic class known as direct thrombin inhibitors, agents that achieve anticoagulation by binding directly to thrombin and preventing the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. This classification highlights their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in conditions requiring precise inhibition of thrombin activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 503
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excluding heparin constitute a pharmacologic class that includes beraprost, indobufen, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. Each of these agents is a member of the platelet aggregation inhibitor group that is used when heparin‑based anticoagulation is not appropriate. By inhibiting platelet activation and aggregation through distinct mechanisms, these drugs help prevent thrombotic events in various cardiovascular and vascular conditions, making them valuable options in patient care where anticoagulation strategies must avoid heparin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1300
retrieved_docs.content: Direct thrombin inhibitors are classified as antithrombotic agents, and both argatroban and dabigatran etexilát are specific drugs that belong to the direct thrombin inhibitor class. By directly inhibiting thrombin, these agents provide anticoagulant effects that are utilized in the prevention and treatment of thrombotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 310
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excl. heparin is a pharmacologic class that includes the agents beraprost, clopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, ticlopidin, and treprostinil. All of these drugs share the common mechanism of inhibiting platelet aggregation while not belonging to the heparin family, making them useful alternatives or adjuncts in antithrombotic therapy for patients who require platelet inhibition without the anticoagulant effects of heparin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 546
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excl. heparin is a pharmacologic class that includes the agents beraprost, epoprostenol, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. Each of these drugs functions to inhibit platelet aggregation through distinct mechanisms, providing therapeutic options for preventing thrombotic events in patients where heparin is not indicated. The class encompasses prostacyclin analogues such as beraprost, epoprostenol, and treprostinil, a P2Y12 receptor antagonist (klopidogrel), a selective IP receptor agonist (selexipag), and another P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor). Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate antiplatelet therapy while avoiding heparin‑related effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 96
retrieved_docs.content: it is used in the treatment of cerebral infarction. Low‑molecular‑weight heparins (enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin, tinzaparin) potentiate antithrombin III‑mediated inhibition of Factor Xa and thrombin. Direct Factor Xa inhibitors (e.g., rivaroxaban) and direct thrombin inhibitors (argatroban, dabigatran etexilate) represent small‑molecule antithrombotic agents that block the final steps of the coagulation cascade. In the ATC classification, several ophthalmological compounds—ethylmorfin, potassium iodide (jodid draselný), riboflavin and the unknown‑mechanism agent merkaptamin—share placement with heparin, highlighting overlapping therapeutic categories. All of these drugs fall under the broader category of “drugs, medicaments and biological substances causing adverse effects in therapeutic use,” together with systemic antibiotics of unspecified type and anti‑helminthic drug poisoning, underscoring the potential for adverse reactions across diverse therapeutic classes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1796
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excluding heparin constitute a pharmacologic class that includes abciximab, beraprost, dipyridamol, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. All of these agents are recognized as members of this class and share the common therapeutic purpose of inhibiting platelet aggregation to prevent thrombotic events, while differing from heparin, which acts as an anticoagulant rather than a direct platelet inhibitor. Their inclusion in the same category underscores their relevance in antiplatelet therapy and highlights the importance of selecting appropriate agents for patients requiring platelet function modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1155
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excluding heparin represent a pharmacologic class of agents that prevent platelet clumping without the use of heparin. Beraprost, dipyridamol, iloprost, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil are all members of this class. By inhibiting platelet aggregation through various mechanisms, these drugs are employed in the prevention and treatment of thrombotic disorders and contribute to comprehensive antiplatelet therapy.
